flags: The summary and description call the roofline 'peaked', but the cut list describes a SINGLE SLOPE: the 75" back legs and 67-7/8" front legs differ by 7-1/8", which is the 10° fall across the 42-5/8" side roof trim. The rewrite follows the cut list and never says 'peak'.
Mattress-cleat height is not specified anywhere in the plan. The rewrite says to set both cleats level and at the same height without asserting a number.

Siding layout is not determined by the data: the 42" side siding matches the 42-5/8" depth, but four 47-1/2" front siding boards do not divide the 75" front width, and the four 4-1/4" 'small front siding' pieces have no stated position. The rewrite describes technique and leaves the front layout to the reader.

Lumber verified with packBoards: twelve 8 ft 2x4, three 8 ft 1x6 and twelve 8 ft 1x8 — matching the material list exactly. The 1x8 packs with ZERO slack (worstSlack 0), so a short board costs a whole extra one.
Step 1's existing cut step is accurate and was left byte-identical.

ops.body: Build two frames, each 75" wide: a back frame from the two 75" back legs and a front frame from the three 67-7/8" front legs. The 10° bevel goes at the top of every leg, all sloping the same way, and the 7-1/8" difference between the 75" and 67-7/8" legs is exactly what produces that slope once the frames stand. Screw a 75" mattress cleat across the inside faces of each frame's legs with 2-3/4" screws, setting both cleats at the same height and checking each is level so the mattress lies flat. Before the last screws go in, measure across the tops of the legs and across the bottoms: both readings must be exactly 75", or the whole structure develops a lean the moment the roof trim and siding add weight.

Stand both frames upright and parallel with the taller back frame behind the shorter front frame, then tie them together at the top with the two 42-5/8" side roof trim pieces, one down each side. Each is cut long point to short point with both ends 10° off square and parallel, so it beds flat on the beveled leg tops and sets the bed's front-to-back depth at the same time. Drive 2-3/4" screws through each trim board into the top of every leg it crosses. Before moving on, check that the gap between the two frames measures the same at both ends and that each frame is still square — everything from here registers off this box.

Attach the two 76-1/2" front/back roof trim boards across the fronts of the front legs and across the back frame. At 76-1/2" they run 3/4" past the 75" frame width at each end, which covers the cut ends of the side roof trim and closes the roofline in. Set the front board low enough that a roof board laid on the 10° slope bears flat along it instead of riding up over its top edge — lay one roof board across it and sight along the slope before you commit, because this is the detail that goes wrong when the trim height is eyeballed. Fasten with 2-3/4" screws driven into the legs behind, not just into the side trim.

Start with the bottom row of side siding — eight 42" boards, four per side, each the 1x8's full 7-1/4" width — setting the first board level along the bottom of the legs and driving 1-3/4" screws into every leg it crosses. Work upward from there and check level on every row: a first row that sits 1/8" out of level compounds into a visibly wedged gap by the time you reach the roofline. Side the front the same way with the four 47-1/2" front siding boards, butting the edges tight rather than spacing them. Cut the four 4-1/4" small front siding pieces last, from your offcuts, to fill the short gaps left around the opening; run any board long and trim it flush afterward rather than trying to land a cut exactly.

Screw the 24-3/4" front mattress trim across the front opening at the height of the mattress cleats with 1-3/4" screws, so the mattress cannot slide out through the opening. Then mount the 31-3/4" ladder step across the front legs, centering it vertically between the floor and the mattress trim so the climb up is one even reach. Fasten the step with 2-3/4" screws driven into the solid 2x4 legs behind the siding — this piece takes a child's full weight, and screws that stop in 3/4" siding will not hold it.

Find the middle of the 75" roofline and set the first of the eleven 45-3/4" roof boards there, running it front to back down the slope, then screw it with 1-3/4" screws into the front 1x6 roof trim underneath. Work outward from that center board in both directions, adding one board to each side in turn and overlapping each new board onto the one before so water and dust shed down the slope. Every screw along the front edge has to land in that 1x6 trim — a screw that misses it holds nothing — so mark the trim's position on the board faces before you start driving. Eleven 7-1/4" boards overlapped cover the 75" length with room to spare, so adjust the overlap a little as you go rather than leaving one wide gap at an end.

Drop the nine 39" mattress slats across the bed onto the two cleats and space them about 5" apart along the 75" length, working from the middle outward so any leftover space lands at the ends. Screw each slat end down into the cleat with a 1-3/4" screw so nothing shifts when the bed is climbed on. Nine slats is a minimum, not a maximum: a thin or foam-only mattress wants closer spacing, and adding a few more is the cheapest insurance against a sagging middle.

Sand every surface a child will touch and ease the sharp corners, paying particular attention to the ladder step, the front mattress trim and the leg edges around the opening. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, working the finish into the siding overlaps where bare edges would otherwise show. Let the finish cure fully before the mattress and bedding go in.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Build the front and back frames
Join the frames with roof trim
Add the mattress trim and ladder step

flags: REAL SHORTFALL: the material note says one 10 ft 1x12 'yields all five shelves', but five 36" shelves total 180" against a 120" board. Verified with packBoards — sides and shelves together need THREE 10 ft 1x12 (1 for the two 56" sides, 2 for the five 36" shelves), not the two listed. The rewrite states the correct board count.

REAL DEFECT: the back panel material is specified as 1/4" plywood 30" x 38", but the assembled case is 37-1/2" wide x 56" tall. That piece cannot cover the back. The rewrite says to buy the back to the assembled size.

Assembly reading is from the plan's own footer step, which pocket-screws the 36" 1x3 through its ENDS into the sides — so the 36" shelves sit BETWEEN the 56" sides, giving 37-1/2" outside width. The box-geometry script reported no determination for this plan; this reading comes from the plan's text, not from a guess.

DERIVED, not authored: shelf spacing. With the top and bottom shelves flush at the ends and three between, the four bays come to roughly 13" of clear height each. The rewrite offers this as derived guidance.
The plan had NO cut step at all; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Cut the case parts
step.body: Cut two sides 56" long and five shelves 36" long from the 1x12, one footer 36" from the 1x3, and four shelf trim pieces 36" from the 1x2; save the trim offcuts for the four optional 9" shelf supports. Every 1x12 part is the board's full 11-1/4" width, so only the lengths change and no ripping is needed. Check the board count before you shop: the two 56" sides fit on one 10 ft (120") 1x12 with about 7-7/8" left, but five 36" shelves do not fit on a single 10 ft board — three fit at 108-1/4" with the kerf and the last two need a second board, so this case takes three 10 ft 1x12, not the two the material list shows. All four 36" trim pieces and all four 9" supports come out of two 8 ft 1x2. Square every cut and label the pieces as they come off the saw.

ops.body: The two 56" sides run the full height and the five 36" shelves fit between them, which makes the finished case 37-1/2" wide (36" of shelf plus 3/4" of side at each end) and 56" tall. The plan's own footer step confirms that reading, since the 36" 1x3 footer is pocket-screwed through its ends into the sides. Mark every shelf position on both sides before you assemble anything, drawing the line on both faces of each joint so you can see where the board lands and where the fastener goes. With the pocket-hole jig, drill 3/4"-setting pocket holes into the undersides of the shelves and drive 2" screws with glue into the sides; without it, screw through the outside face of each side into the shelf ends, with glue either way. Set the top and bottom shelves flush with the ends of the sides and space the middle three evenly — with 3/4" stock that leaves roughly 13" of clear height per bay — and check that both diagonals match, because the back panel and shelf trim will lock in whatever shape you leave.

Measure the assembled case across its outside corners and cut the 1/4" plywood back to that size — the material line calls for a 30" x 38" piece, which is smaller than the 37-1/2" x 56" case it has to cover, so buy the back to your own assembled dimensions rather than to that number. Square the case first: measure both diagonals, rack it until they match, and nail the back on while it is holding that shape. Attach with glue and 3/4" to 1-1/4" brad nails, catching every shelf as well as both sides — nailing into the shelves is what stops the case racking under load, so a perimeter-only nailing pattern wastes the panel's whole structural job.
materials: Plywood, 1/4", 30" x 38" or larger, for back

baseStepTitles: Build the loft bed with one support left out

flags: REAL DEFECT: the cut list's 'Cabinet back' is 58" x 26-1/2", which matches neither dimension of a case built from 55" shelves and 26-1/4" sides (55" wide x 27" tall with the top on). The rewrite tells you to cut the back to your assembled case and names the discrepancy.

The 55" main shelves run the full width and the 3/4" divider sits between them — PROVED by the plan's own numbers: (55" - 3/4") / 2 = 27-1/8", exactly the length of the two adjustable shelves. The box-geometry script found no resolvable box here; this comes from the cut list's own arithmetic.

Door frame geometry likewise closes: two 2-1/2"-wide stiles at 17-1/4" plus a 21-1/2" rail between them = a 26-1/2"-wide door for a 27-1/8" opening, and the 17-1/4" stile matches the 17-1/2" divider height less a gap.
NOT determined: the two 'Door panels' (1x6, 5-1/2" x 26-1/2") have no stated role alongside the acrylic insert. The rewrite does not assert one.
NOT determined: the 29-1/4" legs are taller than the 27" assembled case, so they must run past it as feet, but the plan never says by how much or where they stop.

Lumber verified with packBoards: the eight 3/4" plywood panels pack onto four 15-3/4" x 96" strips, which is where the material note's 'one and a half sheets' comes from. The 1x2, 2x2, 1x3 and 1x6 quantities all cover their parts.
The plan had NO cut step; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Break down the sheets and cut the parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 15-3/4"-wide strips with the grain — three strips come off each 4x8 sheet — then crosscut two sides at 26-1/4", three main shelf and top blanks at 55", one center divider at 17-1/2" and two adjustable shelves at 27-1/8". Those eight panels pack onto four 96" strips (55" plus 26-1/4" twice, then 55" plus 27-1/8", then 27-1/8" plus 17-1/2"), which is exactly the material note's one and a half sheets. From the pine, cut four side trim at 15-3/4", one top back trim at 55" and eight top door area trim at 26-3/4" from the 1x2; six legs at 29-1/4" and four bottom trim at 26-3/4" from the 2x2; and two top breadboard ends at 19-3/4" plus one top front trim at 55" from the 1x3. Leave the doors, the door frame parts and the cabinet back uncut for now — all three are sized to the openings you actually end up with. Label each panel as it comes off the saw, because a 55" shelf and a 55" top blank are indistinguishable once they are stacked.

ops.body: Mark the divider's position from both ends rather than measuring once from one side. With a 55" span and a 3/4" divider, each bay works out to 27-1/8" — which is exactly the length of the two adjustable shelves, so if the divider drifts even 1/8" one of those shelves will not go into its bay. Stand the 17-1/2" divider between the two main shelves, square to both, and fasten it with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws top and bottom. That 17-1/2" length is also the clear height of each door opening, so this one measurement carries straight through to the doors.

Measure the assembled case across its outside corners and cut the 1/4" plywood back to that size. Do not cut to the cut list's 58" x 26-1/2" figure — it matches neither dimension of a 55"-wide case built on 26-1/4" sides, and cutting to it leaves you long on width and short on height. Square the case first by measuring both diagonals and racking it until they match, then tack the back on with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue, nailing into both main shelves and the center divider as well as the two sides. The back is what holds the case square permanently, so get the diagonals right before the first nail goes in.

Measure each door opening for real — nominally 27-1/8" wide by 17-1/2" tall — and build each frame about 1/4" smaller overall than what you measure, because a door built to the plan's numbers instead of your actual openings is the most common reason doors do not hang flush. The cut list's starting point is two 17-1/4" stiles running the door's full height with two 21-1/2" rails fitted between them, which with 2-1/2"-wide stock gives a 26-1/2"-wide door; adjust both figures to your own openings before cutting. Join the frames with pocket screws, check the diagonals, then fasten the acrylic to the back of each frame so it overlaps the frame's inside edge all round. Hang the doors on the Euro hinges, look for an even 1/8" gap on every side, and correct any unevenness at the hinge cups rather than recutting the door — that adjustability is the reason for Euro hinges here.

baseStepTitles: Mark and predrill every pocket hole first

flags: REAL GAP: the tool list is miter-saw and spring clamps only, but five of the six part types are narrower than the board's 3-1/2" width and must be RIPPED (3", 1-1/2", 1", 1/2"). No rip-capable tool is listed. The rewrite names the ripping explicitly and says the tool list does not cover it, rather than pretending a miter saw can do it.
Lumber verified with packBoards: the three crosscut lengths needed (14-1/2", 14-1/2", 8") fit the single 4 ft board with about 10-3/4" spare before ripping.

NOT determined by the data: the vertical position of the 1" top front rail on the 8" back verticals, and where the second pair of 2-1/2" side pieces goes. The plan's own steps use only two of the four side pieces. The rewrite places the remaining two at the upper front corners as the only reading the part count allows, and flags it here.
The rack is assembled with glue and nothing else — no fasteners appear anywhere in the materials. That is the plan's design, not an omission in the rewrite.
This plan is published: false.

ops.body: Everything comes off the one 4 ft (48") 1x4, which is a true 1/2" thick and 3-1/2" wide, so most pieces have to be ripped to width as well as crosscut to length. Crosscut two 14-1/2" lengths and one 8" length first, which uses about 37" of the board and leaves room for a mistake. Then rip: from the first 14-1/2" piece take the bottom at 3" wide; from the second take the bottom front at 1-1/2" and the top front at 1", which sit side by side inside the 3-1/2" width. From the 8" piece rip two back verticals 1/2" wide and two strips 1" wide, then crosscut the four 2-1/2" side pieces out of those strips. Note before you start that the listed tools cover crosscutting only — the rips need a table saw, a circular saw with a straightedge, or a hand plane.

Stand one 2-1/2" side piece at each end of the 3"-deep bottom with its 1" face against the bottom, then glue and spring-clamp them there. These two pieces set the depth of every slot and hold the front rails clear of the bottom, so keep them square to the bottom and flush with its ends — a rack that is out of square holds the boards at a lean inside the cabinet door. Give the glue about 30 minutes before you handle the assembly, and its full cure time before you load it.

Glue the two 8" back verticals upright at the back corners, one against each side piece — at 1/2" square they set the rack's 8" height and are what the cutting boards lean against. Clamp them plumb to the bottom, since any lean here becomes a gap between the rack and the cabinet door once it is mounted. If you can, notch the side pieces to receive the verticals before gluing: it roughly doubles the glue surface on the smallest parts in the whole rack, which is exactly where a glue-only assembly fails.

Glue the 14-1/2" bottom front rail across the front on its 1-1/2" face, then the 14-1/2" top front rail on its 1" face higher up — between them and the back verticals, each cutting board is captured top and bottom instead of leaning loose. Add the two remaining 2-1/2" side pieces at the upper front corners to tie the top rail back to the verticals. Clamp the whole thing and check it stays square and flat across its 14-1/2" width, because it has to sit flush against a cabinet door. Let the glue cure fully before you sand or finish it.

flags: REAL SHORTFALL: the six face frame stiles are 84" long, and an 84" piece cannot come out of a 6 ft (72") board. Only the four 8 ft 1x2 can yield one stile each, so two more 8 ft 1x2 are needed and the two 6 ft 1x2 cannot substitute. Verified with packBoards (six 84" pieces need six 96" boards).

Plywood verified with packBoards: the twenty-two panels pack onto twelve 15-1/4" x 96" strips, which is exactly the four sheets listed — with essentially zero spare (worstSlack 1/2"). The rewrite says so rather than implying a comfortable margin.

Door geometry closes exactly and is stated: two 76" stiles running full height with two 26" rails between them gives 26" + 3-1/2" + 3-1/2" = 33" wide by 76" tall, matching the 33" x 76" panel. The box-geometry script found no resolvable box; this is the cut list's own arithmetic.

NOT determined: the tower shelf layout. Six shelves and a 14" spacer block do not divide an 84" side evenly, so the rewrite keeps the plan's own relative-spacer instruction and does not invent a shelf schedule.
The assembled span (two 33-1/4" towers plus a 65-3/4" console = about 132") is derived arithmetic offered as a sanity check, not an authored number; the plan itself says to cut the face frame to fit.
The plan had NO cut step; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Rip the sheets and cut the parts
step.body: Rip all four 3/4" plywood sheets into 15-1/4"-wide strips on the table saw first — three strips per sheet, twelve 96" strips in all — then crosscut four tower sides at 84", twelve tower shelves at 31-3/4", one center console top and three center console shelves at 65-3/4", and two center console sides at 29". Those parts fill all twelve strips with almost nothing left: the four 84" sides take a strip each, the 65-3/4" pieces pair with the 29" sides on two strips, and the 31-3/4" shelves go three to a strip. There is no margin for a miscut, so measure each strip before you crosscut it. From the 1x3 cut four tower bay supports at 31-3/4" and two center bay supports at 65-3/4"; from the 1x4 cut two door stiles at 76" and two door rails at 26". Edge-band every exposed plywood edge now, while the panels are loose and flat, and leave the face frame and the 1/4" door panels until the case is standing.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-1/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Measure the assembled unit rather than trusting the cut list: two towers at 33-1/4" each plus the 65-3/4" console come to roughly 132", which is where the 11 ft face frame figures come from, but plywood thickness and joint variance add up across an 11-foot span. Cut the two 1x6 top and bottom rails to your measured length, and cut the six 1x2 stiles at 84" — checking your stock first, because an 84" stile will not come out of a 6 ft 1x2, so all six must come from 8 ft boards at one per board, and the material list is two 8 ft 1x2 short of that. Attach with wood glue and 1-1/4" brad nails: one stile over each tower's outer edge, one over each tower's inner edge, and one at each side of the center console. Fill the nail holes and sand flush — the face frame is what hides every small assembly error behind it, so its fit matters more than the case's.

Each door is two 76" stiles running the full height with the two 26" rails fitted between them, which with 3-1/2"-wide stock gives a finished door 33" wide by 76" tall — exactly the size of its 1/4" panel, so the panel doubles as your squareness check. Drill pocket holes in both ends of each rail, assemble the frame flat on the floor with wood glue and 1-1/4" pocket screws, and measure both diagonals before the glue grabs. Lay the 33" x 76" panel on the back of the frame, glue it and nail it off with 1-1/4" brads around the perimeter; if the panel's corners land on the frame's corners, the door is square. Build both doors the same way — together they cover 66" of the roughly 132" span, so they conceal half the wall at a time and slide to expose whichever half you need.

flags: Box geometry per scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs: the 46-1/2" members sit BETWEEN 3/4" members to give 48" outside, proved by the 11-1/4" x 48" bench top. The rewrite states this for both the bench (46-1/2" bottom between 17-1/4" sides) and the shelf (46-1/2" shelf/back between 16-1/2" sides) and never contradicts it.

Lumber verified with packBoards, all four species sufficient: 1x12 (48" + 46-1/2" on the 8 ft; the four side/divider pieces on the 6 ft), 1x8 (both 46-1/2" pieces plus a 16-1/2" and an 8-1/2" on the 10 ft = 118" of 120"; the rest on the 8 ft), 1x4 (six pieces on two 8 ft boards), plywood exactly 24" of 24".
The 1/4" plywood splits 14" + 10" = EXACTLY the sheet's 24" width, so one piece necessarily comes up a saw kerf short. The rewrite says to take the loss on the shelf back, which is hidden.

DERIVED, not authored: the bench bottom's height off the floor. The 13-1/4" divider between the bottom and the underside of the 3/4" seat leaves about 3-1/4" of side below the bottom panel, which the 3-1/2" footer trim covers. Stated as arithmetic, not as an authored number.
NOT determined: the angle to cut on the shelf sides' tops. The plan says to cut one but never says what it is; the rewrite says any angle works as long as both match.
The plan had NO cut step; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Cut the parts for both pieces
step.body: From the 8 ft 1x12 cut the bench top at 48" and the bench bottom at 46-1/2"; from the 6 ft 1x12 cut two bench sides at 17-1/4" and two bench dividers at 13-1/4" — every 1x12 part is the board's full 11-1/4" width. From the two 8 ft 1x4 cut one bench top back trim at 48", two top side trim at 12", two footer sides at 11-1/4" and one footer front at 49-1/2"; those four "cut to fit" pieces are worth running long and trimming once the box is together. From the 10 ft 1x8 cut both 46-1/2" shelf main shelf/back pieces plus one 16-1/2" side and one 8-1/2" divider, which uses 118" of the 120"; from the 8 ft 1x8 cut the 49-1/2" shelf top front, the second 16-1/2" side and the second 8-1/2" divider. Cut the 1x2 shelf top back at 49-1/2". Last, split the 48" x 24" plywood into a 14" x 48" bench back and a 10" x 48" shelf back — 14" and 10" is the sheet's full 24" width with nothing left for the kerf, so cut the bench back to size first and let the shelf back finish about 1/8" narrow, where it is hidden.

ops.body: The two 17-1/4" sides run the full height and the 46-1/2" bottom fits between them, making the carcass 48" wide outside — the same as the 48" bench top, so the top lands flush at both ends. Because people sit on this, join the 1x12s with 2" screws or 1-1/4" pocket screws set for 3/4" stock, not brads alone. Screw the sides to the bottom first, then stand the two 13-1/4" dividers on the bottom between them to split the run into three cubbies; the 13-1/4" divider is the clear cubby height, which puts the underside of the bottom about 3-1/4" up from the ends of the sides — right where the 3-1/2" footer trim will cover it. Check both diagonals for square before the seat goes anywhere near it.

The shelf is built the same way as the bench: the two 16-1/2" sides run the full height and the two 46-1/2" pieces — the main shelf and the hook board behind it — fit between them, giving the same 48" outside width so the two pieces line up on the wall. Before assembling, cut the top of each side at an angle so there is no square corner at eye level; the plan does not specify the angle, so pick one that looks right and cut both sides together, or use the first as a template — matching matters more than the number. Assemble with wood glue and 1-1/4" brad nails, since this piece hangs rather than gets sat on, setting the two 8-1/2" dividers between the shelf and the hook board to break the run into three bays like the bench below. Check the diagonals before the glue sets; the 1/4" back added next will lock in whatever shape you leave.

Attach the 49-1/2" 1x2 shelf top back and the 49-1/2" 1x8 top front with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue. Both run 1-1/2" past the 48" box, so set each to overhang 3/4" at either end and the ends will line up with each other automatically. Then attach the 10" x 48" plywood back with 3/4" brad nails and glue, catching both dividers as well as the sides — it is what keeps the light nailed box from racking once coats hang off it.

baseStepTitles: Finish the shelf and add the top
Mount hooks and hang, then finish

flags: REAL SHORTFALL (1x4): eight panel trim pieces at 72-1/2" each need their own 8 ft board — two will not fit on a 96" board — so eight 8 ft 1x4 are required against the six listed. Verified with packBoards (8 boards, provably one per board).

REAL SHORTFALL (2x4): each 69-1/2" joist needs its own 8 ft board (two do not fit), and only the four 21" legs and two 10" leg pieces are short enough to ride in the leftovers. Working the rest out — 88-1/2" x2 and 78-1/2" x2 alone, 54" + 34-1/2" paired — gives fourteen 8 ft 2x4 against the thirteen listed. packBoards returns 14 (lowerBound 13, not machine-proved), and the by-hand exhaustion above is what the rewrite states.

REAL CONTRADICTION: the cut list calls the panel trim 72-1/2", the existing step text says 'approximately 78-1/2"', and the panel itself is nine 7-1/4" boards = 65-1/4" wide. All three disagree. The rewrite names the conflict and tells the builder to measure, rather than picking one.

NOT determined: the headboard's overall assembled width. The 78-1/2" top rail and 80-1/2" top trim do not reconcile with a 65-1/4" panel plus two 3-1/2" legs (72-1/4"), and the leg lamination's orientation is not stated. The rewrite does not assert a total width.

Lumber that DOES check out (packBoards): the nine 1x8 cover both panels with four boards to spare; the single 1x3 holds all four panel ends at 90" of 96"; the 2x6, 1x6 and 2x10 each cover their single parts.
The plan had NO cut step; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 1x8 cut nine headboard panel boards at 30" and nine footboard panel boards at 15" — three 30" pieces or six 15" pieces per 8 ft board, so five of the nine boards cover both panels. From the single 1x3 cut two headboard panel ends at 30" and two footboard panel ends at 15", which uses 90" of the 96", so measure twice. From the 2x4 cut two headboard inner legs at 34-1/2", two outer legs at 54", two bottom leg pieces at 10", four footboard legs at 21", two top rails at 78-1/2", two side cleats at 88-1/2" and eight joists at 69-1/2". Cut two side rails at 87-1/2" from the 2x10, the headboard top trim at 80-1/2" from the 2x6, and the footboard top trim at 80-1/2" from the 1x6. Two board counts do not work out as listed: each 69-1/2" joist needs its own 8 ft 2x4 and only the 21" and 10" pieces can share the offcuts, which brings the 2x4 total to fourteen boards rather than thirteen; and the eight 1x4 panel trim pieces at 72-1/2" take one 8 ft board each, so eight are needed rather than six. Leave all eight trim pieces uncut until the panels are glued up and measured.

ops.body: Lay the nine 30" 1x8 panel boards edge to edge on a clean, flat surface; at 7-1/4" apiece they make a panel 30" tall and about 65-1/4" wide. Measure that assembled width for real before you cut any trim, because the plan disagrees with itself here: the cut list calls the panel trim 72-1/2" while the build note says roughly 78-1/2", and neither equals the 65-1/4" the panel boards actually add up to. Cut the four 1x4 trim pieces to what you measure across the panel plus whatever the legs add at each side, and confirm that number again once the legs are on. Fasten the trim with wood glue and 1-1/4" brad nails driven from the back of the panel, so no nail holes land on the face that shows.

baseStepTitles: Build the frame and attach the side rails
Attach the frame to the headboard
Attach the frame to the footboard

flags: The existing step 1 named the panels but gave no dimensions at all — the plan's one genuinely under-specified step. Every other step is detailed and was left byte-identical.

Assembly reading from the cut list's own arithmetic: the 46-1/2" bottom shelf sits BETWEEN the two 29" sides (46-1/2" + 3/4" + 3/4" = 48"), matching both the 48" top and the 48" x 29" back, and the 46-1/2" footer blank. The box-geometry script found no resolvable box for this plan; this is arithmetic on the plan's own numbers, not a guess.

Plywood verified with packBoards: the eight case panels pack onto three 20" x 96" strips, and two 4x8 sheets yield four such strips — leaving the fourth strip and the offcuts for the door blanks and drawer boxes, which is consistent with the cut list's 'scrap' material notes.
The 4 ft 1x4 covers the 46-1/2" footer blank with 1-1/2" to spare.

ops.body: Rip your two 3/4" sheets into 20"-wide strips — two strips per 4x8 sheet, four 96" strips in all — then crosscut the case parts: two sides at 29", the top at 48", the bottom shelf at 46-1/2", two divider sides at 24" and two drawer box divider pieces at 14". Those eight parts fit on three of the four strips, which leaves the fourth strip and the offcuts for the door blanks and drawer boxes later. Note how they go together as you cut: the two 29" sides run the full height and the 46-1/2" bottom shelf sits between them, so the case comes out 48" wide outside — matching the 48" top, which caps the sides, and the 48" x 29" back. Cut the 46-1/2" footer blank from the 4 ft 1x4 now, but leave its decorative profile unmarked until it is at the cabinet. Doors, drawers and moulding all get cut to fit later, once real openings exist to measure against.

baseStepTitles: Attach the top, bottom, and divider

flags: REAL SHORTFALL: the twenty-two 2x4 parts need ten 8 ft boards, not the nine listed. The eight 35-1/2" studs take four boards two to a board, and only the 21-3/4" and 22-1/2" blocking fits the ~24-7/8" left on each; the 73", 72", 72", 41-1/4" x2, 31-1/2" x4 and 28-1/2" x2 pieces then need six more. packBoards returns 10 (lowerBound 9, not machine-proved); the by-hand exhaustion above is what the rewrite states.

Frame layout DERIVED from the plan's own arithmetic and stated as such: the front wall is two 72" rails with FOUR of the eight studs between them, because 72" less four 3-1/2" studs = 66" of bay and the blocking is 21-3/4" + 22-1/2" + 21-3/4" = exactly 66". Each side wall then takes the remaining two studs, since 31-1/2" less two 3-1/2" studs = 28-1/2", exactly the side wall center horizontal. The box-geometry script found no resolvable box here.
Wall height DERIVED: seven 5-1/2" pickets = 38-1/2", which equals a 35-1/2" stud plus two 1-1/2" rails. That is what fixes the studs as sitting BETWEEN the rails rather than the reverse.
Cedar pickets verified: seven go on the front uncut at 72", and the other seven halve into the fourteen 35-1/2" side pieces (2 x 35-1/2" + kerf = 71-1/8" of 72"). Exactly 14 pickets, as listed.
2x12 and 1x3 both verified sufficient with packBoards.

NOT determined: how the 83-1/2" front countertop and 41-1/4" side top trim relate to the 72" front wall — the overhang is never stated. The rewrite does not assert one, and step 4 already says to fit the trim to the assembly.
This plan is published: false.

step.title: Cut the framing, siding and countertop stock
step.body: From the 2x4 cut two front top/bottom rails at 72", eight vertical studs at 35-1/2", two front horizontal pieces at 21-3/4", one front middle horizontal at 22-1/2", four side wall top/bottom rails at 31-1/2", two side wall center horizontals at 28-1/2", two side top trim at 41-1/4" and one front top trim at 73". Check the board count before you shop: those twenty-two pieces take ten 8 ft 2x4 rather than the nine listed, because the eight 35-1/2" studs claim four boards two to a board and only the short blocking fits in what is left over. From the 2x12, cut the 83-1/2" front countertop plus one 32-1/2" side countertop off the first 10 ft board, and the 72" inside shelf plus the second 32-1/2" side countertop off the other. Cut six side trim at 35" from the three 6 ft 1x3, two per board. The fourteen cedar pickets divide neatly with no waste: seven go on the front at their full 72" with no cut at all, and the other seven each cut in half to give the fourteen 35-1/2" side siding boards.

ops.body: Lay the two 72" rails parallel on the ground and set four of the 35-1/2" studs between them, one at each end and two spaced inside, which makes the front wall 72" wide and 38-1/2" tall overall. The plan's own numbers confirm that layout: 72" less four 3-1/2" studs leaves 66" of open bay, and the three blocking pieces are 21-3/4", 22-1/2" and 21-3/4", which is exactly 66". Screw through the rails into the end grain of each stud with two 2-1/2" exterior screws per joint, then fit the three horizontals across the three bays at a common height. Check both diagonals before you side it — this frame is the reference that the two side walls, the trim and the countertop all register to.

Nail the seven 72" cedar pickets across the front frame with 1-1/4" brads, starting flush at the bottom rail and working up. At 5-1/2" wide, seven boards cover exactly 38-1/2" — the frame's full height — so butt each board tight to the one below rather than spacing them, or you will run short of coverage at the top. Put at least two nails per board into every stud and rail behind it; brads driven into siding alone will let the cedar cup as it dries outdoors.

Build each side wall the same way from two 31-1/2" rails and two of the remaining 35-1/2" studs, one stud at each end, with the 28-1/2" center horizontal fitted between them — 31-1/2" less two 3-1/2" studs is 28-1/2", so that piece proves the layout is right before you screw it. Stand each side wall against an end of the front wall to form the U, screwing through the side wall's end stud into the front wall's end stud with 2-1/2" exterior screws at the top, middle and bottom. Then side each side wall with seven of the 35-1/2" picket halves, running the courses the same direction as the front so they line up around the corner. The side siding is cut to fit as the walls come together, so hold each board in place and mark it rather than trusting the 35-1/2" figure, and rip the last board's width if needed to land flush at the corner.

baseStepTitles: Build and side the two side walls
Notch and install the inside shelf

flags: MISLEADING MATERIAL ASSIGNMENT (not a shortfall): the cut list assigns all four 49-1/4" front/back trim pieces to the single 10 ft 1x4, which holds only two. Verified with packBoards — the three 1x4 boards ARE sufficient, but only by cross-packing: 10 ft board = 49-1/4" x2 + 14-1/2"; one 8 ft = 49-1/4" + 14-1/2" x3; the second 8 ft = the last 49-1/4". The rewrite states the working split.

TIGHT: four 47-3/4" tabletop boards on two 8 ft 1x8 leaves 3/8" total slack per board once the kerf is allowed (packBoards worstSlack 0.375). The rewrite tells the builder to verify the boards measure a full 8 ft first.

Panel geometry from the cut list's own arithmetic and stated in the rewrite: the 14-1/2" side trim equals the width of two 7-1/4" boards, and the 49-1/4" front/back trim equals the 47-3/4" panel plus 3/4" of side trim at each end — so the side trim goes on first and the front/back trim runs full length over its ends. The box-geometry script found no resolvable box for this plan.
NOT determined: the height at which the inner support (and therefore the bottom shelf) sits on the 23" legs, and the console's overall finished height. The rewrite does not assert either.
2x4 verified sufficient with packBoards: all fourteen 2x4 parts fit the two 8 ft boards.
The plan had NO cut step; one is inserted at the front.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Cut four tabletop boards at 47-3/4" from the two 8 ft 1x8, two per board — that uses 95-5/8" of a 96" board once you allow 1/8" for the kerf, so measure your boards before cutting and start at the squarest end. From the 2x4 cut four legs at 23", two inner supports at 14-1/2" and eight cross supports at 5-1/2" long point to long point, both ends cut at 45° with the ends NOT parallel; both 8 ft 2x4 cover all fourteen pieces. The eight 1x4 trim pieces — four side trim at 14-1/2" and four front/back trim at 49-1/4" — need all three 1x4 boards, and not the way the cut list's material column suggests: the 10 ft board holds two 49-1/4" pieces plus one 14-1/2", one 8 ft board holds a 49-1/4" plus the other three 14-1/2", and the last 49-1/4" takes the second 8 ft board. Cut the trim last regardless, once the panels are glued up and measured.

ops.body: Edge-glue two 47-3/4" 1x8 boards for each panel — at 7-1/4" apiece that gives a panel 47-3/4" long and 14-1/2" wide — running glue along the mating edges and clamping or weighting them flat until dry. Build two identical panels, one for the top and one for the bottom shelf; anything different between them reads immediately on a two-tier piece where they sit one above the other. Measure each glued panel's actual width, then cut the side trim to that measurement (14-1/2" nominal) and nail it across the panel ends with 1-1/4" brads and glue. The front and back trim then runs the full length over the ends of the side trim at 49-1/4" — the 47-3/4" panel plus 3/4" of trim at each end — which caps everything and gives a finished panel 49-1/4" by 16".

Build two leg sets, each two 23" legs joined by a 14-1/2" inner support — the same length as the side trim, so the leg sets come out matching the panel's width. Set the support at the height you want the bottom shelf and drive two 2-1/2" screws per joint into the legs. Then add four of the eight cross supports as corner braces: each is 5-1/2" long point to long point with a 45° cut at both ends and the ends not parallel, so it spans from a leg down to the support at 45° and stops the leg set racking sideways. Fasten those with 1-1/4" brads and glue, checking each leg set sits flat and square on the floor before the glue sets. The remaining four braces stay off until the end, once the top and bottom panels are already joined.

baseStepTitles: Build the top and bottom panels
Attach the legs to the tabletop
